food_subgroup: Battered and fried prawns with salted egg yolk sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for salted egg yolk coated fried prawns. FOODON_03302051 (shrimp food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CJW (Crustaceans, cooked) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for salted egg yolk fried prawns in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226888001 (Prawn) is the closest available substance concept. No specific concept exists for the prepared salted egg yolk dish.

preparation_method: wok-tossed in salted egg yolk sauce
coated with curry leaf and chilli butter sauce

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Golden Salted Egg Yolk Prawns is approximately 200 g, representing a shared portion of 4–6 whole prawns coated in salted egg yolk sauce, suitable for 1–2 persons as part of a multi-dish Chinese meal.

nutrient_source.title: Fried prawns with egg coating — nutrient profile estimated from USDA FoodData Central: Shrimp, coated, fried (FDC ID 175180) combined with salted egg yolk sauce component estimated from USDA FoodData Central: Egg yolk, salted (FDC ID 172185) and butter (FDC ID 173410)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Golden Salted Egg Yolk Prawns exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values are estimated by component-weighted composition: approximately 55% fried battered prawn (USDA FDC 175180), 25% salted egg yolk sauce (USDA FDC 172185 egg yolk salted + butter FDC 173410 + evaporated milk FDC 171278), and 20% batter coating (USDA FDC 168936 cornstarch). Weighted average calculated per 100 g edible portion. This is a medium-confidence estimate.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index estimated by analogy: battered fried seafood / mixed fried seafood dish. Reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Fried battered fish/seafood GI range 38–55 (mean ~48).
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Golden Salted Egg Yolk Prawns. GI of 48 is estimated by analogy with battered fried seafood products (GI range 38–55) from the International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283), which is an approved peer-reviewed source. The relatively low GI reflects the high protein and fat content moderating glucose response. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (48 × 21.0) / 100 = 10.1, using 200 g serving with 10.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g) from salted egg yolk and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption of Golden Salted Egg Yolk Prawns due to its high sodium content (~520 mg/100 g; ~1040 mg per 200 g serving), which represents approximately 45% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; portion restriction and avoidance of additional salty condiments is strongly advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from batter coating with low-to-moderate GI (48); high fat content may impair insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume Golden Salted Egg Yolk Prawns in small portions (100 g or less) given its low-to-moderate glycemic index of 48 and glycemic load of approximately 5 per 100 g serving, but should be mindful of the high saturated fat content (5.6 g/100 g) and elevated cholesterol (185 mg/100 g) which may contribute to insulin resistance and cardiovascular risk when consumed frequently.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (5.6 g/100 g), high cholesterol (185 mg/100 g), and high sodium (520 mg/100 g) from salted egg yolk and deep-frying
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat Golden Salted Egg Yolk Prawns as an occasional indulgence only, as the combination of high saturated fat (5.6 g/100 g), high dietary cholesterol (185 mg/100 g), and high sodium (520 mg/100 g) from the salted egg yolk sauce and deep-frying process presents a compounded cardiovascular risk profile; consumption should be limited to small portions and infrequent occasions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Golden Salted Egg Yolk Prawns (黄金蛋黄虾球, Huáng Jīn Dàn Huáng Xiā Qiú) is a popular Singaporean-Chinese restaurant dish featuring whole prawns that are first coated in a light cornstarch batter and deep-fried until crispy, then wok-tossed in a rich, aromatic sauce made from salted duck egg yolks, butter, evaporated milk, curry leaves, and chilli. The dish is characterised by its signature golden-yellow colour, a crispy-tender texture contrast (外酥里嫩), and a deeply savoury, slightly briny, and mildly spicy flavour profile. The salted egg yolk sauce clings to each prawn ball, creating a foamy, buttery coating that is intensely umami. Some restaurant versions include a hint of mustard for added complexity. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense at approximately 218 kcal per 100 g, with a moderate protein content of 14.2 g/100 g from the prawns, and a high fat content of 13.8 g/100 g driven by the butter and egg yolk sauce. Sodium is elevated at approximately 520 mg/100 g due to the salted egg yolk. The glycemic index is estimated at 48 (low-to-moderate), reflecting the moderating effect of protein and fat on glucose absorption. Clinically, this dish is of concern for individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or hypercholesterolaemia due to its high sodium, saturated fat, and dietary cholesterol content. It is not suitable for individuals with crustacean, egg, milk, or gluten allergies. In Singapore, this dish is commonly served as a shared main course in zi char (煮炒) restaurants and is a staple of celebratory and family dining occasions.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Golden Salted Egg Yolk Prawns was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated by component-weighted composition from USDA FoodData Central approved entries for fried battered shrimp, salted egg yolk, butter, and evaporated milk. GI is estimated by analogy with battered fried seafood from the International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values (Atkinson et al., 2008). Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-weighted estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central approved sources (FDC IDs 175180, 172185, 173410, 171278, 168936). No direct laboratory analysis or primary database entry exists for this dish. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component sources used for estimation. 3. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate based on battered fried seafood from Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283; no direct GI measurement exists for this dish. 4. The food_group has been corrected from the input value of 'Dairy and eggs' to 'Fish and seafood' as the primary ingredient and nutritional character of this dish is seafood (prawns), with salted egg yolk as a sauce component. 5. Halal status is marked false as salted egg yolk prawns prepared with butter may use non-halal certified dairy; restaurant-specific halal certification should be verified. 6. The dish contains mustard as noted in the input description; mustard is listed as an allergen. 7. trans_fat_g of 0.1 g/100 g reflects trace trans fats from butter used in the sauce, consistent with USDA data for butter.
